theorem_statement:
Glassey blow-up argument for the focusing mass-critical nonlinear Schrodinger equation.

Consider a sufficiently regular solution \(u(t,x)\) to the focusing mass-critical NLS on \(\mathbb{R}^d\):

\[
i\partial_t u+\Delta u=-|u|^{4/d}u.
\]

Assume:

- \(u_0\in H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)\),

- \(|x|u_0\in L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)\),

- the energy \(E(u_0)\) is negative, where

\[
E(u)=\frac12\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|\nabla u|^2\,dx
-\frac{1}{2+4/d}\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|u|^{2+4/d}\,dx.
\]

Then the maximal forward existence time \(T\) is finite and

\[
\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty
\quad\text{as }t\uparrow T.
\]

nodes.title: Finite-time blow-up from negative energy
nodes.informal_statement: Under the stated assumptions, the maximal forward existence time \(T\) is finite and \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty\) as \(t\uparrow T\).
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Let \(V(t)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\). By the virial computation, \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\), so \(V\) is a concave quadratic with negative leading coefficient. Since \(V(t)\ge 0\) by definition, it cannot stay nonnegative for all large positive \(t\); hence there is a finite positive time \(T\) with \(V(T)=0\), and therefore \(V(t)	o 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\). By the weighted inequality applied to \(f=u(t)\) and conservation of mass, if \(M=\|u_0\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\), then \(M^2\le  rac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|
abla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\). Because \(M>0\) is fixed and \(\sqrt{V(t)}	o 0\), it follows that \(\|
abla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}	o\infty\) as \(t\uparrow T\). Since \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1}^2=\|u(t)\|_{L^2}^2+\|
abla u(t)\|_{L^2}^2\) and the \(L^2\) norm is conserved, we obtain \(\|u(t)\|_{H^1(\mathbb{R}^d)}	o\infty\). Thus the solution blows up in finite positive time.

nodes.title: Virial identity and negative variance curvature
nodes.informal_statement:
For \(V(t)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\), one has
\[
V'(t)=4\operatorname{Im}\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}x\cdot \nabla u(t,x)\,\overline{u(t,x)}\,dx
\]
and
\[
V''(t)=16E(u(t))=16E(u_0)<0.
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Differentiate \(V(t)\) once:
\[
V'(t)=2\operatorname{Re}\int |x|^2u_t\overline u\,dx.
\]
Using \(iu_t+\Delta u=-|u|^{4/d}u\), rewrite \(u_t=i\Delta u+i|u|^{4/d}u\). Substituting into \(V'(t)\), the nonlinear term vanishes because \(\int |x|^2|u|^{2+4/d}\,dx\) is real, so its product with \(i\) is purely imaginary. Integrating by parts in the Laplacian term gives
\[
\int |x|^2\Delta u\,\overline u\,dx
=-2\int x\cdot \nabla u\,\overline u\,dx-\int |x|^2|\nabla u|^2\,dx,
\]
and the last term is real, hence disappears after multiplying by \(i\) and taking real parts. Therefore
\[
V'(t)=4\operatorname{Im}\int x\cdot \nabla u\,\overline u\,dx.
\]
Differentiating again and substituting \(u_t=i\Delta u+i|u|^{4/d}u\), \(\overline{u_t}=-i\Delta\overline u-i|u|^{4/d}\overline u\), one splits \(V''(t)\) into linear and nonlinear contributions. A standard integration-by-parts computation yields for the linear part
\[
4\operatorname{Re}\int x\cdot \nabla\Delta u\,\overline u\,dx-4\operatorname{Re}\int x\cdot \nabla u\,\Delta\overline u\,dx=8\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|\nabla u|^2\,dx.
\]
For the nonlinear part, after rewriting the real-part contribution as a divergence term and integrating by parts using \(\operatorname{div}(x)=d\), one gets
\[
-\frac{16}{2+4/d}\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|u|^{2+4/d}\,dx.
\]
Hence
\[
V''(t)=8\int |\nabla u|^2\,dx-\frac{16}{2+4/d}\int |u|^{2+4/d}\,dx=16E(u(t)).
\]
By conservation of energy, \(E(u(t))=E(u_0)\), so \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\).

nodes.title: Weighted inequality relating mass, variance, and gradient
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Using \(\operatorname{div}(x)=d\), integrate by parts:
\[
\|f\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}^2
=\int |f|^2\,dx
=\frac{1}{d}\int (\operatorname{div}x)|f|^2\,dx
=-\frac{1}{d}\int x\cdot \nabla(|f|^2)\,dx
=-\frac{2}{d}\operatorname{Re}\int x\cdot \nabla f\,\overline f\,dx.
\]
Then \(|\operatorname{Re} z|\le |z|\) and Cauchy-Schwarz imply
\[
\|f\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}^2
\le
\frac{2}{d}\,\|x f\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\,\|\nabla f\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}.
\]

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: The theorem uses \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\) to force the variance to hit zero in finite positive time.

edges.label: depends_on
edges.explanation: The theorem combines this inequality with mass conservation and \(V(t)\to 0\) to conclude \(\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2}\to\infty\).
